summary: Today, May 25th, I am in Seattle, Washington, where I live near Boulevard Park. I've been here for about 22 years with my son. Seattle has been my home base while I work and support my family in Mexico.

I live in the Boulevard Park area, which is close to Burien and above South Park. I've been living in this building here for about five years. Boulevard Park is where I have built my life while working multiple jobs to support my family.

Burien is a nearby area to where I live in Boulevard Park. It's part of the greater Seattle area where I work and manage my daily life. I often mention Burien when describing my neighborhood and surroundings.

I am Jakar Delacruz, a student at the University of Washington. I conducted this interview here in Seattle, and my connection to the university is as a student. The university is part of the context in which this oral history was recorded.

I was born in Mexico City, Mexico, and my roots are deeply Mexican. I have lived in the U.S. for over two decades, but Mexico City remains my cultural and familial home. I send money back to my family there because the economic situation is difficult.

Mexico is my home country where my parents and sisters live. Even though I have lived in the U.S. for 22 years, I still support my family there financially. The economic hardship in Mexico affects my ability to help them, especially during the pandemic.

The COVID-19 pandemic affected me deeply; I was suspended from work for a whole year and had to find other jobs to survive. I faced many challenges, including irregular hours, low pay, and health risks at work. I also had two car accidents during this time, which made working even harder.

I was not able to receive any help from the CARES Act because I don't have legal immigration status or a social security number. This lack of access to federal aid made it very difficult to cover rent and other expenses during the pandemic. Many others in my situation also missed out on this support.
